So, for example, the first entry is:

This means that the mutant in DateTimeZone.java at line 68, code snippet 1, caused a fault adjacent to it. So the output should include each of these as separate entries.

I think I need to create an array where each object has "mutant_id" and "is_fault_adjacent". Since all the provided mutants have is_fault_adjacent set to true, except maybe none in this case, but according to the input, all are true.

Wait, no. Actually, looking back, each entry in the user's input has a boolean value of true or false. For instance, some might be "src-main-java-org-joda-time-DateTimeZone-68-1": true and others as false. But in their provided data, all seem to have true.

"src-main-java-org-joda-time-DateTimeZone-68-1": true,
"src-main-java-org-joda-time-Interval-52-1": false,

So, in my output, I need to include each of these as separate objects within an array.

Wait, but the user's example shows only one entry. Hmm, perhaps they want all mutants that are causing adjacent faults, each as a separate object in an array.

But looking at their sample input and output format, it seems like the output should be an array of such objects.

So I need to parse each key-value pair from the provided data and create an array where each entry has "mutant_id" as the key and "is_fault_adjacent" as the boolean value.

In this case, all the mutants in the input are adjacent faults except none. So for example:

[
  {
    "mutant_id": "src-main-java-org-joda-time-DateTimeZone-68-1",
    "is_fault_adjacent": true
  },

]

Wait, but in the user's provided data, each key is a mutant ID and the value is whether it's adjacent. So I can create an array where each entry corresponds to one of these.
